> Are there any critical fields that are in the event stream that are not in the results backend?

Some of the more relevant things that are missing in the results backend:

- task name
- task inputs
- worker that executed the task
- runtime
- retries

And you don't have anything on tasks that have been revoked, and on tasks that ignore their result.

> Reconstructing state from event stream seems more difficult than just grabbing state explicitly from the results backend if it's available.

Task state reconstruction doesn't need to be reimplemented, you just "replay" the relevant events and Celery does it for you the way it usually would. However, if we talk about analyzing this data by software other than Flower; task object reconstruction is not always necessary -- as these are often (in our case) related more to what was happening on the cluster than the specific results of tasks; so a timeline of events is more useful (or if you need the results, you can get them from "task-succeeded" events).

At the moment, the biggest issue with my approach is that the in-memory task object cache has problems. Most notably, when the LRU cache ejects an item, the task heap is not updated; so you get unexpected behavior when displaying those tasks in Flower. In our case, it is not a problem if only recent tasks are accessible with Flower while all events are persisted (and available for offline analysis); so the current in-memory model of Flower is not an issue, but the current in-memory caching mechanism would need to be improved (which is something I plan to try doing as soon as I get the chance).

I've already implemented it--it was pretty straightforward.  In a nutshell, the task suspends itself. A separate thread picks it up and continues the task by making a single call to the coroutine `.send()` method to make it to advance through the block to the `__aexit__()` method.  From there, it gets put back on its original thread.  No async/await/yield operations are allowed inside so there is no possibility of suspension or interaction with other parts of curio while this is happening.   There is no result from a context manager.   This does not work by submitting the body of the context manager somewhere and waiting for it (no concurrent execution of the context manager with any other part of the function).

Honestly, it's mainly a shortcut for doing this sort of thing:

    async def coro():
           def sync_code():
                    do_something()
                    do_something_else()
         run_in_thread(sync_code)

Of course it's absolutely not pythonic. Infact I've been waiting for 
this response.. ;) I proposed it as general solution for the other
issues related with task/exception management, queues, links,
mailboxes and so forth.
Anyway, it is not a big deal for me to use it where it makes really
good sense. This issue serves as for historic record.. Thanks.

On 11/18/16, Nick Coghlan <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> I see the status quo in curio as somewhat similar to the old
> bytecode-counting based switching model for CPython's GIL - it was mostly
> OK, but would sometimes go horribly wrong if you were calling a lot of
> computationally intensive C code that held on to the GIL without calling
> back in to Python code. People's solution to that was sticking
> `time.sleep(0)` at various points in their code as explicit yield points,
> but switching out the bytecode-counting based pre-emption for time based
> pre-emption was still a good idea.

Hey @shadow7412 ,

You can definitely send direct messages to users.  However, the 'subscribe' functionality you describe would be custom code you'd write yourself.

To send 1-1 messages, you'll want to use `self.send_direct_message(hipchat_id, content, **kwargs)`.

It isn't currently part of the publicly exposed API (typical use-cases have involved will replying to 1-1 messages, instead of proactively sending them), but I'm open to it becoming publicly exposed.

If you're interested in writing that feature, let me know, and we can spec it out!

The interface would be something like:

```python
self.send_private_message(user, content, html=False, color="green", notify=False)
```

I think the documentation was confusing in regards to *lexicographicMode* option. I've just [reworded](http://pysnmp.sourceforge.net/docs/hlapi/asyncore/sync/manager/cmdgen/nextcmd.html#getnext-command) it what would hopefully explains the behavior you observe.

In short -- "*lexicographicMode = True*" walks the MIB till the very end, otherwise walk is capped by the initial OIDs (which serve as prefixes). You can change those prefixes at any time by feeding the new ones back into running generator (via its `.send()` method).

Overall I believe this is a great feature to add, but it's not an obvious easy feature, and to be honest it's not in the ballpark for the next few releases (except if you implement it and push a PR!).

That being said, I believe there should be four feature requests, the first two that are almost trivial to implement, the third one would be quite a headache, and the last one should be done another way.

So I guess I'll have to split this issue in 4 new tickets!

## ① defaults for git-repo XXX create

For that feature I believe we should use something that makes it clear that we're talking about default values for create. So I guess I'd see more something like:

```
[gitrepo "myprecious"]
    type = gitlab
    token = YourSuperPrivateKey
    fqdn = gitlab.example.org
    create_repo_visibility = public
    create_repo_description = Fubar
    create_repo_merge_style = squash
    … 
```

## ② Implement default configuration values and inheritence

Once the former has been implemented, this new feature makes sense:

```
[gitrepo "default"]
   create_repo_allow_push_master = none
   create_repo_merge_style = squash
   create_repo_visibility = public
   create_repo_description = this is a super awesome project that will solve all of your problems
```

and shouldn't be hard to implement, as configuration is being checked in those places:

* https://github.com/guyzmo/git-repo/blob/master/git_repo/services/service.py#L100,L119
* https://github.com/guyzmo/git-repo/blob/master/git_repo/services/service.py#L140,L158

## ③ implement "binding" between repository services

that one is a tougher one. I guess that your `all` service could be considered as a new `gitrepo "all"` section in the configuration, that would be configured with the `mirror_*` actions you're suggesting, something like:

```
[gitrepo "all"]
  mirror_create = github,gitlab,bitbucket
  mirror_gist = github,gitlab
  mirror_delete = github
  mirror_request = github
```

I'm not sure about the `mirror_push` and `mirror_pull` options, because that's to handle that use case that we got a `all` remote.

My only issue with that feature, is that it's a bit too meta, and implementing this might complexify the code base a bit too much, the tests will be a headache. So I'd rather keep that feature on the side once we got more lower level features implemented first.

## ④ changing the settings

About your `update` command idea, I'm not really for that. Firstly because I believe that having defaults for `git XXX create` is a good idea, and secondly becuase I'd rather see a `settings` command (with get/set) that would make it easy to change a repository's configuration:

```
git XXX settings user/repo ls
git XXX settings user/repo get key
git XXX settings user/repo set key value
```

The point of Domovoi is to provide an easy way to hook up Lambdas to various events in AWS. To that end it reuses a lot of the machinery that Chalice developed for packaging and deploying Lambdas, and turns off the parts that it doesn't need. The short term way to do what you want is to deploy one app with Chalice, another app with Domovoi, and throw code that's shared between the two into chalicelib/domovoilib.

What you describe is possible, but would require a bunch of changes to how Domovoi patches Chalice behavior. It also has the potential to unnecessarily couple both the deployment process and the entry point behavior of Domovoi to the semantics of API Gateway and the Chalice entry point that it overloads.

Chalice has limited support for mixing "pure Lambda functions" with API Gateway handlers. Chalice authors have also put a lot of thought into making Chalice do more event handling of the kind that Domovoi allows:
- https://github.com/aws/chalice/issues/604
- https://github.com/aws/chalice/issues/516
- https://github.com/aws/chalice/issues/390

I'm not convinced this _should_ work. From the python documentation ([2](https://docs.python.org/2/library/marshal.html) and [3](https://docs.python.org/3/library/marshal.html))

> The marshal module exists mainly to support reading and writing the “pseudo-compiled” code for Python modules of .pyc files
> Not all Python object types are supported; in general, only objects whose value is independent from a particular invocation of Python can be written and read by this module. The following types are supported: ....
> If you’re serializing and de-serializing Python objects, use the pickle module instead ... pickle supports a substantially wider range of objects than marshal

It _appears_ to work in python 2 only because `unipath.Path` is a subclass of `AbstractPath` which is a subclass of `unicode`. Unmarshalling does not work in python 2:

```
>>> marshal.loads(marshal.dumps('/hello/world'))
'/hello/world'
>>> marshal.loads(marshal.dumps(unipath.Path('/hello/world')))
'/\x00h\x00e\x00l\x00l\x00o\x00/\x00w\x00o\x00r\x00l\x00d\x00'
```

Additionally, python 2 just treats a string subclass as a string and throws away any extra attributes:
```
import marshal
class ExtraString(unicode):
    def __init__(self):
        self.extra = 5

>>> marshal.dumps(u'') == marshal.dumps(ExtraString())
True
```

Finally, this isn't specific to `unipath`, you can't marshal arbitrary class instances:
```
>>> marshal.dumps(object())
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
ValueError: unmarshallable object
```

In short: the problem is actually that python 2 is silently accepting something it shouldn't

Oh, and further information that might be useful to you

CERT_NONE, CERT_OPTIONAL, CERT_REQUIRED

These options are to do with how ssl.py validates the certificate information associated with the certificate if receives *from the other end*

So in pygelf.py it is to do with server cert validation, but if you were to use ssl.py to create (say) some TLS network service, then you'd use the same vars to validate the client cert. "Normal" web servers would default to the equivalent of CERT_NONE, and as a counter-example, we run Apache web servers that require the browser to have a client cert, so they'd use CERT_REQUIRED or CERT_OPTIONAL (that one is useful as it allows the transaction to complete - but you can then generate an nice HTML error page - compared with CERT_REQUIRED where the user would just get this horrible browser SSL error that means nothing to them)

Ah.  It isn't strictly necessary, though it still might be a good idea from a usability point of view.

ODBC only allows one prepared statement per HSTMT (Cursor), so you can simply create one cursor for each SQL statement you want to prepare.  They are prepared automatically if there are parameters and reused if you execute with the same (exactly the same) SQL.

It might be useful for two reasons: (1) some developers are familiar with prepared statements in other languages and (2) you don't have to keep the Cursor *and* the SQL, just the cursor.

If I have to prepare anyway to get the parameter type info, it might be better to maintain the old API to keep the surface area down.

Thanks, @jakobandersen. I think it would be great to have some kind of hooks in the C++ domain that we could use instead of the internal APIs. Currently we extend the nodes generated by the Sphinx C++ domain in the following way:

1. Insert a target (anchor) with the ID generated by Doxygen.
2. Modify the main signature node adding qualifiers and keywords like `virtual`, etc.
3. Add description to the content node.

For example:

```python
        node = nodes[1]
        # Templates have multiple signature nodes in recent versions of Sphinx.
        # Insert Doxygen target into the first signature node.
        node.children[0].insert(0, self.create_doxygen_target())
        # Update the last signature node because it contains the actual signature
        # rather than "template <...>".
        self.update_signature(node.children[-2])
        # Add description to the content node.
        node.children[-1].extend(self.description())
```
where `nodes` are Sphinx nodes obtained by running a cpp domain directive. Obviously this is rather fragile. One way to make it more robust is by locating the nodes we need by tags rather than by position, but ideas of how to improve this are welcome.

This can be changed by setting USE_RTTI=1 when doing the "make" in rocksdb.

Probably, this settings change isn't done in the CMake version of rocksdb's build system, which is why that works as a a workaround too.

But @fozzold's suggestion is probably most useful for python-rocksdb, i.e. don't rely on rtti being present.

I didn't think Alexa is allowed to just "speak" without being asked  for
something. I believe it can only speak in response to a request. So whilst
you can do the "started" bit I don't think you can do the finished without
asking Alexa yourself "Alexa has the task finished".

You could possibly fudge the experience by using the audio API and play a
blank 10s mp3 on loop and stop looping once the task has completed at which
point you could return a voice response (see the playlist sample). This
works as a song finishing play triggers a request for Alexa on what to do
next. This obviously has the downside that you will be in audio mode and as
such if you do anything else audio you won't get your finished (E.g. asking
to play music after you kick your task off)

(Note: obviously it is possible to make Alexa just speak - E.G. Amazon do
this for the timer. I don't believe this is exposed to other developers
over the API).

If you write commands as a simple list, then how do you plan to provide a frequency when base64 is used? You'd still need to make a dict with with code and frequency. And for Pronto you'd want to be able to provide number of repeats.

I wonder if the autodetection should be on the library side or on the HA component side. You have much more control if you do this in the component and then just call the right method in the library.

The Pronto code in the example I've provided in the comment:
```
0000 006C 0022 0002 015B 00AD 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0041 0016 0041 0016 0041 0016 0041 0016 0041 0016 0041 0016 0041 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0041 0016 0016 0016 0041 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0016 0041 0016 0016 0016 0041 0016 0016 0016 0041 0016 0041 0016 0041 0016 0041 0016 0623 015B 0057 0016 0E6E
```
is the one I've used to generate the base64 version you've tested successfully in #175. You can either use it in a format like above for readability or you can remove the spaces. If you remove the spaces then the character set used in the code is the subset of charset used in base64. Of course there really low chance that this would be an actual base64 string, especially because the binary form of the signal starts with the magic sequence 0x67, 0xa5, which should always produce a base64 string starting with "Z6".
So the simplest and most naive method of autodetection would be to check if the string starts either with "Z6" or "0000" and report an error if anything else is provided.

Instead of autodetection you could also go with something like this: `raw:BASE64_STRING[:OPTIONAL_FREQUENCY]` and `pronto:PRONTO_STRING[:OPTIONAL_REPEAT_COUNT]` and parse it in the component and then pass to correct method. The color character is not allowed in neither base64 nor pronto so such format would be safe to use and future proof in case the library would add support for another IR format.

When the map is created first, the starting location of the coordinates (where the robot sits at this moment) are always [25000,25000] and the rest of the map is then build around those coordinates. 
On the app you can then share/save the map (once it's complete). Use the "app_goto_target" command to figure out how much your robot moves with each coordinate change. Then create a grit with those coordinates on your image of the map and you can start creating areas/rooms. In theory - since the robot always starts from the same point and the map should not change as long as you don't move the station, this should work. It's a little bit of work, but other users from OpenHab/iobroker have reported that it works great. They had issues with the map randomly rotating 90 degrees, but that seemed to only affect a few people.

It has to do with using ``numpy.array`` to do conversions such as on lines 116 and 202 in [0.1.x/tests/asserts.py](https://github.com/frejanordsiek/hdf5storage/blob/0.1.x/tests/asserts.py). ``` python
``` python
numpy.array(2**31).dtype
numpy.array(2**60).dtype
```

On 64-bit Python 3.x, the returns are ``dtype('int64')`` and ``dtype('int64')``

On 64-bit Python 2.x, the returns are the same as for 64-bit Python 3.x

On 32-bit Python 3.x, the returns are ``dtype('int32')`` and ``dtype('int64')``

On 32-bit Python 2.x, the returns are the same as for 32-bit Python 2.x


Essentially, on 32-bit Python, Numpy converts ``int`` to either ``numpy.int32`` or ``numpy.int64`` depending on the size. But in 64-bit Python, Numpy always converts it to ``numpy.int64``.

Hello,
Sorry for answering so late. I have very little internet connectivity atm.
Would it be possible to have a look at your signal handler?

My guess is this issue arises like this:
  1) calling `save` saves the shared model to database, so…

    a) your `pre_save` handler is invoked
    b) the shared model is inserted
    c) your `post_save` handler is invoked

  2) then the translation is saved to database

This can be a problem if your `post_save` hook manipulates the database. For instance, if it saves the model again, the translation will be saved twice. Other issues may arise as well, including some with other modules.

The best bet would probably be to manually generate an update from your `post_save` instead of saving the model again. By using `Model.objects.filter(pk=instance.pk).update(field=value)`.

Hope it helps.

This is not directly a SuperFences issue. SuperFences currently sends code through the `pymdownx.highlight` extension to highlight code. It will use [`Codehilite`](https://python-markdown.github.io/extensions/code_hilite/`) options if you've configured that as your highlighter, or it will use its own options if you've explicitly configured [`pymdownx.highlight`](https://facelessuser.github.io/pymdown-extensions/extensions/highlight/). Anyways, both `Codehilite` and `Highlight` use `Pygments` to do highlighting. It is responsible for the output. `Pygments` has two line number output formats, `table` (which is the default) and `inline` where it just integrates the line numbers into the `pre` containing the code. Each have their pros and cons. `table` allows for sane copy and paste and has bad line wrapping, while `inline` allows for wrapping but has bad copy and paste. From Pygments' [documentation](http://pygments.org/docs/formatters/#HtmlFormatter): > If set to 'table', output line numbers as a table with two cells, one containing the line numbers, the other the whole code. This is copy-and-paste-friendly, but may cause alignment problems with some browsers or fonts. If set to 'inline', the line numbers will be integrated in the `
` tag that contains the code (that setting is new in Pygments 0.8).
>
> For compatibility with Pygments 0.7 and earlier, every true value except 'inline' means the same as 'table' (in particular, that means also True).
>
> The default value is False, which means no line numbers at all.
>
> Note: with the default (“table”) line number mechanism, the line numbers and code can have different line heights in Internet Explorer unless you give the enclosing `
` tags an explicit line-height CSS property (you get the default line spacing with line-height: 125%).

Both `Codehilite` and `Highlight` use the `table` output, and are generally expected to use an overflow that allow scrolling instead of wrapping.

You are free to not use Pygments by disabling Pygments usage by setting the `use_pygments` option to `False` and instead configure a JavaScript highlighter that outputs in a format you like. When Pygments is **not** being used, the output of code blocks will be in the HTML format:

```html


```

If you have enabled `linenums`, all it will currently do is set a `linenums` class. We do not provide information like starting line numbers, step, etc.  This is because JavaScript highlighters all require different formats, there is no one, standard way, which makes it hard to provide a universal output.

```html


```

Hi, @wuhuanyan 

`if_exists` was designed as extension of [`table.drop` syntax](https://github.com/xzkostyan/clickhouse-sqlalchemy/blob/7fe83865ef0139dc44fca0b6d0f16b02e1644adf/clickhouse_sqlalchemy/sql/schema.py#L8).

When SA reflect table from database (see query in clickhouse log `DESCRIBE TABLE test_drop FORMAT TabSeparatedWithNamesAndTypes`) it constructs instance of default Table class. That's why `drop.if_exists` in `visit_drop_table` doesn't work.

If we comment or change this string to something like this: `if getattr(drop, 'if_exists', False)`. We will get following error:

```python
~/clickhouse-sqlalchemy/clickhouse_sqlalchemy/drivers/base.py in post_create_table(self, table)
    286 
    287         if not engine:
--> 288             raise exc.CompileError("No engine for table '%s'" % table.name)
    289 
    290         return ' ENGINE = ' + self.process(engine)

CompileError: No engine for table 'test_drop'
```

Engine is required parameter for table creation. I think it also should be reflected from database via parsing `SHOW CREATE TABLE testing` query.

In current pandas [to_sql interface](https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/version/0.22/generated/pandas.DataFrame.to_sql.html) it seems impossible to pass additional parameters such as CH Engine.

I'd recommend you not to drop/create table as temporary solution. Or you can drop/create tables via sqlalchemy. See [README](https://github.com/xzkostyan/clickhouse-sqlalchemy#basic-ddl-support) for examples.

Good question. Most migrations should be handled just fine. The only migration that may cause issues is changing the name of a field that you track with django-field-history. That would mean that:

1. You'd need to change the field name that you pass into `FieldHistoryTracker`, i.e. `field_history = FieldHistoryTracker(['new_field_name'])`.
2. Every `FieldHistory` row would need its `field_name` attribute changed to the new field name. This is definitely the bigger issue of the two. I could probably provide a management command to update `FieldHistory.field_name` based on the `FieldHistory.content_type`.

@nextghost I never said with `UPDATE` the record will be released earlier, I said that I see no point to **lock it earlier**. Again on your example: If you use a `SELECT FOR UPDATE` in thread A, and somehow the thread hangs before proceeding with the `UPDATE` and `COMMIT` or `ROLLBACK`, the thread B won't be able to perform the `UPDATE`. Practically speaking: if something goes wrong in one of the two threads, in one case you have chance to complete at least one operation, in the other one you will miss them both. That's all.

```python
if row:

    return None
return db1.foo.insert(bar='foo')
```

is more correct than this:

```python
updated = db1(foo.bar == 'foo').update(bar='bar')
if not updated:
    return db1.foo.insert(bar='foo')
return None
```

under your point of view.

@app.route('/webhooks/grafana', methods=['OPTIONS', 'POST'])
@cross_origin()
@auth_required
def grafana():

    hook_started = webhook_timer.start_timer()
    alerts = parse_grafana(request.data)

    LOG.warning(alerts)

    for incomingAlert in alerts:

        LOG.warning(incomingAlert)
        if g.get('customer', None):
            incomingAlert.customer = g.get('customer')

        add_remote_ip(request, incomingAlert)

        try:
            alert = process_alert(incomingAlert)
        except RejectException as e:
            webhook_timer.stop_timer(hook_started)
            return jsonify(status="error", message=str(e)), 403
        except Exception as e:
            webhook_timer.stop_timer(hook_started)
            return jsonify(status="error", message=str(e)), 500

        webhook_timer.stop_timer(hook_started)

    if alert:
        body = alert.get_body()
        body['href'] = absolute_url('/alert/' + alert.id)
        return jsonify(status="ok", id=alert.id, alert=body), 201, {'Location': body['href']}
    else:
        return jsonify(status="error", message="insert or update of grafana check failed"), 500

def parse_grafana(check):
    check = json.loads(check)
    evalMatches = check['evalMatches']
    timeout = 60

    alerts = []

    for item in check['evalMatches']:
        LOG.warning(item['metric'])
        alerts.append(Alert(
            status='open',
            resource=item['metric'],
            event=check['state'],
            environment='Production',
            severity='critical',
            service=[check['ruleName']],
            text="Value:" + str(item['value']),
            origin='Grafana',
            event_type='Grafana_event',
            raw_data=item['value'],
            timeout=timeout,
            value='Error',
            group='group'
        ))

    return alerts

This workaround seems to work for me, maybe you an adapt it to your needs:

```python
class Monkey(object):
    def patch(self):
        from selenium.webdriver.firefox.firefox_binary import FirefoxBinary
        from subprocess import check_output
        import os
        def find_firefox_path(self):
            firefox_path = check_output(["mdfind", "kMDItemFSName = Firefox.app"]).decode('utf-8').strip()
            return os.path.join(firefox_path, "Contents/MacOS/firefox-bin")
        # FIXME: only on mac
        FirefoxBinary._get_firefox_start_cmd = find_firefox_path
Monkey().patch()
```

Also. If you have a really big set of keywords, the resulting tree will be very recursive:

From the python [docs:](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pickle.html#what-can-be-pickled-and-unpickled)

> Trying to pickle a highly recursive data structure may exceed the maximum recursion depth, a RecursionError will be raised in this case. You can carefully raise this limit with sys.setrecursionlimit().

So you can raise the recursion limit for now. But if you provide me an example I will have a look into improving the pickling by replacing reference recursion with indices or something like that.

No, it’s about you are using an incorrect invocation style.

On macOS (and Windows, but that is not relevant in your case), files have a “default association”. If you open a file without specifying an executable, it is run with a default one specified by the system. If you install Python with an official installer from python.org, it sets the association automatically.

This means that when you invoke `pipenv run test.py` without specifying an interpreter, the file will always be executed by the default associated executable, which is always a system-wide Python, never the virtual environment Python. To get the interpreter inside the environment, you should write `pipenv run python test.py`. With your commands, the virtual environment is never taken into consideration.

The problem is that `pipenv run --help`  can be (and is) interpreted as “run the command `--help` inside venv” because `--help` is totally a valid command name. Okay so there So a couple of findings. (All of the followings assume Windows.)

1. Pipenv does not handle non-ASCII entries well *at all*.
2. Some additional parsing is required to handle quoting well. This works:

```toml
[scripts]
foo = "python -c print('foo')"
```

but this does not

```toml
[scripts]
foo = "python -c \"print('foo')\""
```

because `shlex.split` does not handle unquoting. This would be a problem if there are paths need escaping.

```python
TEST_VERSION_STRING = "~=2.7/~=3.3"

from typing import List, Tuple
from operator import itemgetter
from pkg_resources import Requirement, Distribution


def get_version_tuple(version_string: str) -> Tuple[int, int, int]:
root, major, minor = version_string.split('.')
return (root, major, minor)


def find_critical_points(version_string: str) -> List[Tuple[int, int, int]]:
"""Find all of the critical points in a version string

A "critical version" is a version at which a higher
or lower distribution version will not satisfy
the requirement. For example, "~2.7" has critical
versions at "2.0", "2.7", and "3.0". We need to exclude
or include ranges between these critical points
"""

# Filter out characters we don't care about
for c in ['<', '>', '=', '~']:
version_string = version_string.replace(c, '')

# Split the string along and/or operators
substrings = version_string.replace('/', ',').split(',')
critical_points = [get_version_tuple(v) for v in substrings]
# Add the root versions
upper_bound = max(critical_points, key=itemgetter(1))[0] + 1
lower_bound = min(critical_points, key=itemgetter(1))[0]
critical_points += [
(v, 0, 0) for v in range(lower_bound, upper_bound)]
return list(sorted(set(critical_points), key=itemgetter(1)))


def get_or_expressions(version_string: str) -> List[str]:
"""Split the version string by the "or" delimeter
Note that we assume here that that a version_string with
"or" expression cannot have "and" expressions because parentheses
are not allowed at this time.
"""
assert ',' not in version_string
return version_string.split('/')


def get_version_below(version: Tuple[int, int, int]) -> Tuple[int, int,
int]:
"""Get the version slightly below the given verion"""
root, major, minor = version

if minor != 0:
return (root, major, minor - 1)
elif major != 0:
return (root, major - 1, 99)
elif root != 0:
return (root - 1, 99, 99)
else:
raise NotImplementedError()
def generate_exclusions_for(
lower: Tuple[int, int, int], upper: Tuple[int, int, int]) -> List[str]:
"""
Generate "and" expressions to exclude all versions between
the lower and upper bound
"""
expressions = []
lower_root, lower_major, lower_minor = lower
upper_root, upper_major, upper_minor = upper
for root in range(lower_root, upper_root):
expressions += ["!={}.*".format(lower)]
# We should technically cover major versions under
# the lower root version, but I will leave that out
# for now, as that has ill-defined behavior
for major in range(0, upper_major):
expressions += ["!={}.{}.*".format(upper_root, major)]

# And we won't even touch minor versions at this points
return expressions


def convert_version_string(version_string: str) -> str:
"""Convert a version_string with "or" expressions to "and"
Note that the expression cannot have "or" and "and"
"""

and_expressions = []
```

FIXED rng seed:
* Pros: 
  * 100% reproducible tests
  * better to spot platform numeric instability
  * one suspect off when "something misbehaves"
  * max determinism
* Cons:
  * no meaning in "restarted local search", aka. no improvement in running twice the same and observing results
  * will not spot possible perf. breakage due to "bad luck" in rng seed selection, aka we can just be lucky with this seed, but other seed might screw up. 

In conclusion, I'd go with a fixed seed (for testing, 99% users), and in special case one can always set to `random seed -1`

I agree with @jirikuncar. A Python list is an ordered collection, and thus order should be taken into consideration when diff'ing two lists. If order doesn't matter then either a set should be used or the lists should be preprocessed (i.e. sorted) prior to being diff'ed. It will make the algorithm overly complex and likely introduced subtle errors to add this feature. 

I understand your issue, but perhaps another solution is to simply create another package with your preprocessor function that relies on diff to do the job? IMHO it's best to keep a algorithm strict and not add all sorts of exceptions into it.

But there are two parts I am not happy about:
- we do not render `TemplateResponse` (changed that to more Django-like checking for `render` method instead of `TemplateResponse`), but render can raise exception that should be processed by middleware, we may refuse doing that for sake of being backward compatible
- Django now always converts exception to response - we do not do that (and we never had) when old-style middleware are used. Problem is that new-style middleware actually relies on fact that this is done (middleware always await response and should not catch any exceptions). So now old-style and new-style differs in that aspect, hard to say how bad this is.

More tests probably needed.

This doesn't work because it clears the temporary index. Indeed, the process of reindexing is as follow:
- clean a temporary index, "%s_tmp" % index_name
- push all records to the temporary index
- move the temporary index to the index (override)

By doing this way, there is no downtime for all search during the reindexing.

If you don't care about this, you can replace the function with this one:

```python
def reindex_all(self, batch_size=1000):
    '''Reindex all records.'''
    if self.settings:
        self.__index.set_settings(self.settings)
        logger.debug('APPLY SETTINGS ON %s', self.index_name)

    result = None
    counts = 0
    batch = []

    if hasattr(self, 'get_queryset'):
        qs = self.get_queryset()
    else:
        qs = self.model.objects.all()

    for instance in qs:
        if self.should_index:
            if not self.should_index(instance):
                continue  # should not index

        batch.append(self._build_object(instance))
        if len(batch) >= batch_size:
            result = self.__index.save_objects(batch)
            logger.info('SAVE %d OBJECTS TO %s', len(batch),
                        self.index_name)
            batch = []
        counts += 1
    if len(batch) > 0:
        result = self.__index.save_objects(batch)
        logger.info('SAVE %d OBJECTS TO %s', len(batch),
                    self.index_name)

    return counts
```

This will reindex all records without temporary index, and without cleaning the index.

An operation is [conceptually a `Additional information about a potentially long-running operation with which a log entry is associated.`](https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/api/ref_v2beta1/rest/v2beta1/LogEntry#LogEntryOperation).  On the entry, the operation is identified by a `producer` ID and a unique ID for the operation, along with flags signalling whether it is the first or last entry in the operation.  I don't know what happens if entries tied to the same operation are delivered out-of-order.

If we wanted to expose it as a wrapper around `Batch`, we could derive `Operation` from `Batch`, and have it inject the correct values onto each entry as it added them to its request.  I don't have a sense how useful this would be for applications which wanted to use the feature:  maybe they would not want to be tied to entries delivered via a single API request?  To support that case, we would need to plumb the `operation` paramter through to the `log_text`/`log_struct`/`log_proto` methods of `Logger` and `Batch`, and expect the user to pass a mapping (like `http_request`) which had the correct fields.

Hmm, maybe we should add a helper for `http_request` (and `operation`) to assist the user in constructing those mappings?

Hi @scanny 
When this feature implementation on main repo?

Using the `Paragraph.text` property to replace text is convenient, but a bit of a brute force method. All the text formatting is specified at the run level, and it all gets nuked when you assign to `Paragraph.text` because that call removes all the existing runs before adding a single new one containing the assigned text.

It's a pretty hard problem in the fully general case, but what I usually do that works well almost all the time is to remove all the runs in the paragraph except the first one, and set its text to what I want. Generally, the first run is formatted the way you want, in my experience at least.

```python
def set_cell_text_while_retaining_text_formatting(table_cell, text)
    # ---replace text of first run with new cell value---
    runs = table_cell.text_frame.paragraphs[0].runs
    runs[0].text = text
    # ---delete all remaining runs---
    for run in runs[1:]:
        r = run._element
        r.getparent().remove(r)
```

This has been a hard-learned lesson.. started with just wanting to fix a bit of robustness here and there, then realizing that there were a lot of challenges.

 * We want clear and simple developer guidelines for releasing stuff
 * We don't like git conflicts because of version bumps
 * We can't trust `git describe` to do all the work without being more strict about how to tag.

And personally, I don't like developers having to go down a deep rabbit hole and relearn all this, every time they want to release something or in case we have weird bugs showing up later.

I've discovered [this guide from Vumi - How We Do Releases](http://vumi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/how-we-do-releases.html), which I think is a really good recipe for describing how to do releases, just manifesting all the practical steps. I don't believe Vumi has solved the git conflict parts (they auto-edit lots of files with `sed` in file replacements).

I'm about to wrap this up by authoring "the correct way of releasing" in the same fashion as Vumi (and to avoid `git describe` problems that we had). Then, I think, we can merge this.

I'm working on a longer-term fix, but it's complicated by my inability to replicate. I'm working with the AdWords team to get access to accounts that can better replicate your scenario. That said, I do think it's likely that the underlying issue is in [googleads.adwords.ReportDownloader._DownloadReport](https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/blob/master/googleads/adwords.py#L1585).

If you need a work-around in the short term, there are two approaches I would suggest. The first would be to patch _DownloadReport such that it downloads the report contents in chunks. The second would be to modify how you're downloading reports--e.g. if a smaller date range is used, the reports may not be large enough to trigger the MemoryError.

An implementation of the former may look like:

```
def _PatchedDownloadReport(self, post_body, output, **kwargs):
    CHUNK_SIZE = 16 * 1024
    response = None
    try:
      while True:
        chunk = response.read(CHUNK_SIZE)
        if not chunk: break
        output.write(chunk.decode() if sys.version_info[0] == 3
                     and getattr(output, 'mode', 'w') == 'w' else chunk)
    finally:
      if response:
        response.close()
```

About the two things mentioned by @miguelsousa, quoting from our conversation with @behdad:

1) for GPOS, makeotf generates multiple PairPos format=2 subtables to reduce size of the table, whereas fontmake (via fontTools.feaLib) doesn't.

2) fontmake already supports CFF subroutinization (via compreffor); however, the FontTools `T2CharStringPen` (originally from Tal Leming's ufo2fdk) doesn't have a CFF operator specializer yet (see https://github.com/fonttools/fonttools/issues/403). In other words, it only uses `rmoveto`, `rlineto`, `rrcurveto` operators, whereas makeotf optimizes by also using `hmoveto`, `hlineto`, etc. So the size of a non-subroutinized CFF table made by makeotf is smaller than one produced by ufo2ft.

> as I suspect deis is not be able to log from all the process simultaneous logs from all processes

logspout is the component behind the log aggregation. It bind-mounts the host's docker socket, listens for events and pipes all container logs over to deis-logger, so the platform and application logs should all be there. Since we provision it as a global unit, *every* host's docker container logs are shipped if everything is working correctly.

If you want logs directly from a container, `deis logs` retrieves the application logs from ceph (deis-logger is backed by cephfs). If you're not seeing any logs, then I'd inspect what logspout and logger are up to. If they're down then logs won't get shipped.

This sounds like a really interesting use-case, but I don't see any simple way to leverage existing Globus tools or services to do it.
If I narrow my thinking to just what's relevant to the SDK, we're concerned with making it easy to use existing Globus services -- this therefore doesn't seem like a great fit. You might be well served by popping off an email to outreach@globus.org describing your use case.

It also might be worthwhile to chat about this on developer-discuss@globus.org (which is meant for stuff like this).

Given that there isn't anything actionable for the SDK on this, I'm going to close it.

---

However, some thoughts before calling it quits...

- Assuming that users will (on purpose or by mistake) violate any fixed schema for data which you set, either (a) all of your tools need to be ready for that or (b) you need to ensure that you aren't trusting users' claims.
  (b) is the easier of the two (after all, what good is a schema if it can be violated...).

- Searchable Globus Endpoint fields are all freely manipulable except for owner.
  Since most of the searchable fields can be written by anyone, and are just text fields, you can't trust endpoint Keywords or Organization values to mean anything. They may still be useful _clues_ about where to look for data, but they aren't verified at all.

- If you want data to be available on demand and durable, you probably need to aggregate it on a central endpoint.
  Globus Connect Personal often represents someone's laptop. Laptops can be offline, Globus Connect Personal could be uninstalled (crazy, I know!), or the machine can be dipped in the nearest liquid nitrogen tank.

- You can't split `find /` across Globus endpoints. This is another reason to centralize things -- I think it will make searching data much easier.
  Globus Transfer doesn't support any `find`-like operations, but even just `ls` on a single endpoint is simpler than searching a disparate (perhaps undefined) set of endpoints, any of which may go offline at any time.

- It sounds like you want to do policy enforcement on the actual data stored on an endpoint. Globus can't do this -- at least not today -- but you might be able to manage it by clever use of the filesystem on an endpoint.
  Imagine this scenario: you have three folders, with different permissions settings (via Globus Sharing ACLs):
  - `RW /writebin`
  - `R /badbin`
  - `R /goodbin`

  You wait for writes to `/writebin` using `inotify` or similar, and then process them with a validator. (Or, easier, you run your validator as a cron job.)
  Well formed docs go to `/goodbin`, malformed docs go to `/badbin`, which gets wiped periodically.
  In this scenario, `/goodbin` only has docs that passed your validation, `/badbin` is only useful for debugging.

Ok, here are the results. The first plot shows a comparison with 1, 4 or 10 processes between v7 and master, with different number of ids. As you see, we are faster on master than with v7 - the difference is larger with larger time series (as then parallel processing is more important) and smaller with larger number of processes (as the execution time is then so fast, that streaming becomes more important, which is more or less the same in both cases, as we do have to transport the data to the processes anyhow).

![parallel](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6116188/28872380-36e2cc62-7789-11e7-93e0-6cf613aafd8e.png)

The next plot shows the speedup plot - so the time with the same number of ids over the number of processes. As you see, we are getting faster with more processes (expected) and with very large number of ids (100) on many cores (10), the speedup is actually worse - the reason is again the streaming, because here the master process can not feed the data as fast as the child processes can work on it (but 100 ids is a corner case with 10 processes...)
![speedup](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6116188/28872448-88ba350c-7789-11e7-9809-25ab14fcea87.png)

Thanks for your contribution @nperraud.

Two remarks:
* Style of docstring. They have a fixed structure: e.g. Parameters, Examples. You did it correctly for the SensorLarge but not for the functions in `learning.py`.
* We don't want to impose the use of FLANN without explicit user choice. For one, they might not have FLANN installed (it's not possible to install it with pip on Windows for example). It's better for users to not have "magic" changes of behavior, like changing from exact to approximate FLANN at arbitrary `N=2000`. If they find it slow, they should enable FLANN, knowing the downsides.

I believe that it is possible to change anaconda environment within the PowerShell, I mainly use cygwin so I am using similar technique to get it working.

Every shell have its own set of environmental variables such as PATH, when a shell is started it inherit the system and user environment variables from Windows.

The activate script on Windows only work on old command prompt because the script add the path of a new environment to the old command prompt's environmental variables.
If you tried to execute the activate command on the PowerShell, the script works but it DOES not affect the PowerShell's environmental variables.
So you can't use the new environment in PowerShell however, the solution is to write a PowerShell script that adds the anaconda environment to the PowerShell environmental variables instead.

Here how I 'activate' a new anaconda environment in PowerShell manually:

	PS C:\> python -V
	Python 3.4.1 :: Anaconda 2.3.0 (64-bit)
	PS C:\> $env:Path = "D:\Anaconda3\envs\py27;D:\Anaconda3\envs\py27\Scripts;" + $env:Path
	PS C:\> python -V
	Python 2.7.10 :: Anaconda 2.2.0 (64-bit)
	PS C:\>

What I did is to add the path of the new environment and its Scripts directory to the Path of the PowerShell.
I have not tested this properly but the version of python I am using changed so hopefully this works for you.

To change back to old environment you have to restart powershell to clear its environmental variables.

Hope this make sense for you!

This is something *big* that we've fixed in 4.3.  Which we released to defaults today!!!

The best recovery method is to download the latest miniconda installer and install it on top of your current environment.

For OSX python 3.5, this would be something like

```
curl https://repo.continuum.io/miniconda/Miniconda3-latest-MacOSX-x86_64.sh -o ~/miniconda3.sh
bash ~/miniconda3.sh -bfp ~/anaconda
```

If you have conda installed in a `~/anaconda` directory.

Not that I know of. To prevent the $finish from being executed automatically, you can add this code before the run_test() code line in tb_top.sv. So, the control is going back to the Vunit SV runner. Still, I see this only as a work-around for development purposes. Btw, Vunit still returns a fail here, so this needs to be investigated.

`uvm_root root;`
`root = uvm_root::get();`
`root.finish_on_completion = 0;`

 Its not related to #293, which deals with recognizing known failures and flagging them to be seen as an OK instead of a FAIL.
 The requirement here is that Vunit does not assume any PASS/FAIL on its own, and the evaluation of the ERRORS is left completely to an external Non-vunit mechanism.

> Is the parsing functionality part of UVM or is this something that the simulator vendors provide? Is this the only way to find out the status of a test run? Is there no way to programmatically ask UVM for the number of errors?

UVM does not do any parsing of the transcript. It has an internal counter/registry which keeps track of all the UVM_<> messages issued within the testbench. Using that, its possible to query this within the UVM/SystemVerilog code towards the end (in a post-simulation check phase) in order to evaluate a PASS/FAIL for the test. This is left to the user
The transcript parsing is done outside the UVM/SystemVerilog code after the simulation actually exits. Some vendors provide support commands for it, but there's no established mechanism. By specifying rules/patterns, the user can parse the transcript for error/warning/fatal messages (also those which are not issued by the UVM world and do not contain UVM_<> tags). This also allows the user to 'exclude' certain error messages which are actually known failures and shouldnt contribute to a PASS/FAIL decision (similar to #293)

BTW, I have two more UVM fundamentals in the list of differences in the first message. . Please have a look.

In this case, you can (and should) still use generic update. The difference is that rather than reflecting on the SDK's create_or_update command for the setter, you would point it to a custom setter. The custom setter would accept the instance (which has been modified by any custom arguments and  the generic arguments) and then populate the individual parameters in the call to create_or_update.  For example:

```Python
def custom_setter(client, resource_group_name, instance_name, instance):
   return client.create_or_update(resource_group_name,
                                  instance_name,
                                  prop_a=instance.prop_a,
                                  prop_b=instance.prop_b,
                                  ...)
```

This does actually seem like something that should be improved, I'll keep my thoughts here so it's not entirely linked to a specific PR.

pre-commit at the core is a developer tool, it's aim is to help developers eliminate quick mistakes and help them, not get in the way.  When used as a pre-commit/pre-push hook its goal _isn't_ to be the last line of defense (there are easy ways to bypass its checks, both with `SKIP=...` and with (dreaded) `--no-verify`).  If you want pre-commit to be a last line of defense, it makes more sense to set it up either as a `pre-receive` hook or in [CI](http://pre-commit.com/#usage-in-continuous-integration).

Because of this, we can probably cut some corners on `pre-push` to bring the filenames down to a more reasonable count for the cases listed above.  Here's some of my thoughts on this (again, only talking about the fallback case where it's currently defaulting to the entire history):

- We can't assume origin names or upstream branch names (this means `origin/master` is out)
- We could use upstream branch
    - upstream can be missing: fall back to slow
    - upstream could be the same branch name: probably ok? it would compare the changed against the same branch itself
    - upstream could be a non-master branch: this is probably fine too, it's a pretty common workflow to branch off a non-master branch and a good comparison for what is being pushed is just the difference between the current commits and that
    - upstream is based on master: I think there's no qualms from me about comparing against origin/master

In conclusion, yeah let's do _something_ about this!  I see this as being very similar to the current optimizations done for [pre-commit during merge conflicts](http://pre-commit.com/#pre-commit-during-merges)

So there's actually one more subtle problem to solve here:  a subdomain's history in the set of zone files can get reorganized with the arrival of *any* previous zone file.  This has a profound consequence for the security of subdomains: **a subdomain can be stolen or invalidated through a reorg.**

Here's how.  Suppose I successfully create `jude.blockstack.id`, and I transfer it from address `1AA` to `1BB`.  My subdomain history might look like this:

```
jude.blockstack.id seq=0 address=1AA zonefile=XXX accepted=1 parent_index=5
jude.blockstack.id seq=1 address=1BB zonefile=XXX accepted=1 parent_index=12
jude.blockstack.id seq=2 address=1BB zonefile=YYY accepted=1 parent_index=19
```

Here, the `parent_index` is the index into the blockchain-ordered list of zone files that identifies the zone file that included the subdomain operation.  For the sake of this example, the zone file at `parent_index=5` was from a `NAME_UPDATE` on `blockstack.id`, so this history is valid.  The zone files at `parent_index=12` and `parent_index=19` do not have to be from `blockstack.id`, per this issue.

Now, here's the problem.  Suppose the subdomain transfer at `seq=1` is the result of me selling the subdomain to a different person.  Suppose now that I want to *defraud* this person.  I can easily do so by sending a `NAME_UPDATE` at any `parent_index=6` through `parent_index=11` whose zone file includes a *different* `seq=1` subdomain record for `jude.blockstack.id`.  I do not announce this zone file at first, so the transfer to address `1BB` is accepted for some time.

When I want to defraud the recipient, I send my alternative subdomain record and reorganize this subdomain's history.  Here's what it might look like if the missing zone file was added at `parent_index=7`:

```
jude.blockstack.id seq=0 address=1AA zonefile=XXX accepted=1 parent_index=5
jude.blockstack.id seq=0 address=1CC zonefile=ZZZ accepted=1 parent_index=7
jude.blockstack.id seq=1 address=1BB zonefile=XXX accepted=0 parent_index=12
jude.blockstack.id seq=2 address=1BB zonefile=YYY accepted=0 parent_index=19
```

Since the subdomain record at `parent_index=7` transfers the subdomain to `1CC`, it *invalidates* the history at `parent_index=12` and `parent_index=19`.  A query to `jude.blockstack.id` would now return `address=1CC` and `zonefile=ZZZ`, whereas before it would have returned `address=1BB` and `zonefile=YYY`.  This is obviously a problem.

**Solution**:  My proposed solution to this problem is to allow a subdomain record to set a list of domain names that can issue transfers.  They would be set when the subdomain is created, and when the subdomain address is changed.  Then, an address change would only be accepted into the subdomain's history if *each* domain name's full zone file history was already present (i.e. to prove the absence of any missing address changes).  This could be used to prevent me from defrauding the user simply by omitting `blockstack.id` as an allowed domain name.

Here's how the history might look before my attempted reorg attack, but with this solution applied:

```
jude.blockstack.id seq=0 address=1AA zonefile=XXX accepted=1 parent_index=5 care_of="foo.id,bar.id"
jude.blockstack.id seq=1 address=1BB zonefile=XXX accepted=1 parent_index=12
jude.blockstack.id seq=2 address=1BB zonefile=YYY accepted=1 parent_index=19
```

The record at `seq=0` states that only the names `foo.id` and `bar.id` are allowed to change the address of the subdomain `jude.blockstack.id`.  That way, when I send my subdomain update at `parent_index=7`, the history would look like this:

```
jude.blockstack.id seq=0 address=1AA zonefile=XXX accepted=1 parent_index=5 care_of="foo.id,bar.id"
jude.blockstack.id seq=0 address=1CC zonefile=ZZZ accepted=0 parent_index=7 care_of="blockstack.id"
jude.blockstack.id seq=1 address=1BB zonefile=XXX accepted=1 parent_index=12
jude.blockstack.id seq=2 address=1BB zonefile=YYY accepted=1 parent_index=19
```

My history entry would be rejected because it did not come from either `foo.id` or `bar.id`.

Note that this still requires that the system know about *all* of `foo.id` and `bar.id`'s zone files.  Otherwise, they could reorganize the subdomain history and defraud the user.

I'm thinking that a subdomain user can set the `care_of` list to at most five different names.  This gives the user some choice in who they can contact (only one of them has to succeed in sending a `NAME_UPDATE` to include their address change), and it keeps the number of database queries we'd need to do server-side to a manageable amount.

Unfortunately there's nothing that pytest-cov can do about this. This is a problem created by the combination of coverage's legacy handling of filters (it mixes up paths with module names in configuration) and problematic package layout (see [1](https://hynek.me/articles/testing-packaging/) and [2](http://blog.ionelmc.ro/2014/05/25/python-packaging/)).

IOW, you cannot filter by module if there's a directory with same name in CWD - sadly that's just how coverage works. Other workarounds (besides fixing the layout) could be:

* changing cwd during test run (not sure if this really works)
* remove filters (as in diff) and filter and fix paths with subsequent `coverage combine` commands (or contraption of that sort)
* use development installs (https://tox.readthedocs.io/en/latest/config.html#confval-usedevelop=BOOL) - note that this has some pitfalls (you're not testing what users will actually install)
Is that the whole `.coveragerc`? Note that you're omitting conftests so there wouldn't be any data for those files anyway.

Could you provide more context, or a minimised test case that reproduces this issue? It would also be useful to know what versions of pytest and the plugins you're using. A full console log would include this.

As for why it's enough to just install `mod_ssl`, @jdkasten updated the Apache plugin to install all of the dependencies manually. The reason for this is that while `a2enmod ssl` will enable all necessary dependencies, `a2dismod ssl` only removes `mod_ssl`. Therefore, if we want to enable `mod_ssl` for `auth` and revert all of our changes when we are done, we have to do the dependency check ourselves. The plugin now enables each module individually and registers a call to `a2dismod` for that module with the `reverter`.

**tl;dr**: calysto_scheme works as described by @dsblank. %kernel matlab_kernel still fails with all 3 engines (native, pymatbridge, octave).

Thank you so much for helping me get this to work. ipython and jupyter are the latest version from pip (`4.1.1` and `5.1.0`, respectively). I installed **calysto_scheme** and can get your example to work without issues. By now, I checked all combinations of the conda environment from which I started ipython (python2.7 vs 3.4) and the kernel from which I tried to make the connection to Matlab.

To further narrow down the problem, I installed **Octave** and set the `OCTAVE_EXECUTABLE` to force matlab_kernel to use octave. Opening Octave (both gui and cli) works fine. After manually doing `from pymatbridge import Octave` this gives me the exact same error message (complaining about missing .metakernel logger and then the error quoted in my last message). However, pymatbridge does not seem to support octave on windows (`octex = os.environ.get('OCTAVE_EXECUTABLE'); Octave(octex).start()` causes a windows-popup complaining about missing liboctinerp-3.dll to open).

Finally, I checked with **pymatbridge** (by commenting out [these lines](https://github.com/Calysto/matlab_kernel/blob/master/matlab_kernel/kernel.py#L29-L31) in matlab_kernel/kernel.py. pymatbridge itself (as well as its matlab-magic) works ok. However, as a backend for matlab_kernel, I can no longer get it working at all. on `jupyter console --debug --kernel matlab` I see the pymatbridge's Window opening up in the background as usual. However, the console crashes with some `TypeError: <StringIO.StringIO instance at 0x0000000007A52688> is not JSON serializable` (full traceback in a [gist](https://gist.github.com/Foucl/e948d900e26541459b392d040fcc0ed7)).

To be honest I feel writing the code for starting a separate process basically amounts to redoing the work that was done in pymatbridge, so it's not worth it (you may as well fix pymatbridge and get to support older versions of everything, then).

I also had a quick look at the other engines MATLAB provides.  The C API (which you could call through ctypes -- everything is in `libeng.so`) is basically useless as it outputs to a `char*` buffer instead of stdout (http://www.mathworks.com/help/matlab/apiref/engevalstring.html).  The Java API, however, provides the ability to pass a *Writer* to `eval` (http://www.mathworks.com/help/matlab/apiref/com.mathworks.engine.matlabengine-class.html#bvarmmm-1) (basically the equivalent of a writable file-like in Python); additionally, a quick test showed me that the output is written in real time to the Writer (rather than everything at once as the Python engine does).

So a strategy to support Windows would be to call the MATLAB Java engine (e.g. via https://pypi.python.org/pypi/javabridge), open a pipe or socket, let the engine write on one side and the kernel read on the other side.  No need for `dup2` anymore!

To be honest I'm not going to bother.  One of the reasons is that async support for any of the engines is weak anyways: for example, whether using the Python or the Java engine, something like
```
start(timer('timerfcn', 'disp(42)'))
```
does not output anything (even without messing up with the redirection) -- probably because MATLAB has closed stdout for this task by the time `disp(42)` is reached.

Sounds like an interesting project! In short: unfortunately folium cannot do this, though it could be an interesting addition.

It's import to understand that folium generates the html with [Leaflet](http://leafletjs.com/) javascript and after that is finished. So anything that happens on the map after generation is outside of folium's scope.

Now there have been ideas of getting callbacks from javascript back to python, mainly for in the Jupyter environment. I can imagine something like that could also work for Flask or Django, where some javascript ajax calls retrieve new data. This is not available in folium though, but of course you're welcome to work on adding this.

folium works great for creating ad hoc maps with python data. For your application I would suggest not to use folium but to use Leaflet directly. You need a fixed map layout and very good integration with your web application, not to create various maps with differing Python data, which is folium's strong point. Of course you can use a map created by folium as inspiration for how to use Leaflet of course.

But that's my opinion. Hope it helps! Maybe others will chime in as well.

> doesn't have to be just two levels by the way

Of course not!

> I'll try to think of some use cases for stateful `train()`.

Well, it depends on what level of abstraction `train()` is :) Something has to log the error history, to be able to do early stopping or implement some learning rate heuristics.

> `fit()` should probably be the top level, but what sits below that is definitely up for debate as far as I'm concerned.

Yes, we need to think this through. I don't have a lot of time right now, so I can just offer some random thoughts:
* It'd be nice to have something that you just pass your network to, along with two numpy arrays, and have it trained. This could be `fit()`.
* This would consist of compiling the training and possibly validation function, and running the training loop.
* Compiling the training and validation functions involves setting up variables for the targets, getting the expression for the network output, setting up the loss and updates, and compiling the functions. This could be `compile_functions()` or something, and offer to compile any of training, validation and prediction functions.
* Running the training loop involves running an inner training loop, computing the validation error, and possibly adapting some hyperparameters, for multiple epochs.
* The inner training loop involves iterating over the training data, calling the compiled training function for a given number of steps, and returning the average training loss.
* We might want to take care not to get carried away. It doesn't have to solve everything. But we may want to ensure we can extend it in the future until it can solve everything :)

Regarding `PathHierarchy`, it started out as an internal structure to support LAZY_SOLVES, and has been getting adapted over time to other applications. The biggest use-cases of PathHierarchy are LAZY_SOLVES and Veritesting, and neither actually requires the full hierarchy (step by step), but just need to reason about where states branched. To minimize overhead, PathHierarchy simplifies itself to collapse chains of non-branching states. `PathHierarchy.simplify` is a function that helps do that.

One of the side-effects of it is that it "demotes" the PathHistories that it removes from itself in the process of this simplification, and clears their strong state references so that states can be garbage-collected. This makes sense from its point of view, but is unexpected when people are using those references. It's a collision of several features that's now caused weird behavior.

I think the most elegant way to do this is to change strong_references to have three modes:

- `True`: strong references are always kept, even after PathHistory simplification
- `False`: strong references are never kept, even before PathHistory simplification (this will break LAZY_SOLVES and Veritesting, but is the right thing for applications such as rex)
- `None`: default behavior - strong references are kept until they're wiped out during PathHistory.simplification.

Would it make more sense to implement FunctionFeaturizer using scikit-learn's `TransformerMixin` subclass (as in [PolynomialFeatures](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.preprocessing.PolynomialFeatures.html#sklearn.preprocessing.PolynomialFeatures)) and not the `BaseFeaturizer` ?

Having FunctionFeaturizer as a BaseFeaturizer seems a little bit unnatural. When using a model, I would have to keep track of multiple Featurizers, the order in which I call them, and that each featurizer takes different data. I can't just stick them all in to a MultiFeaturizer and forget about it.

While this 'negative' about having FunctionFeaturizer as a BaseFeaturizer is somewhat aesthetic (as we do not yet many applications that assume a ML model has exactly one featurizer), I'd also argue that implementing with the `TransformerMixin` base class will allow us to use the FunctionFeaturizer with GridSearchCV and other tools designed to perform hyperparameter optimization with sklearn.


Some other thoughts:
- Should the columns being operated on be stored as a class attribute? This way, you can call `feature_labels` with its current API and without having to modify `featurize_dataframe`.
- I think it would make sense to cite [Ghiringhelli 2015](https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.114.105503) in addition to/in place of the npj review paper. AFAIK, the Ghiringhelli PRL was the first use of this feature expansion technique

I think your Jupyter kernel maintains the global state, meaning that the variable is already created in TensorFlow default graph in the previous cell.
We have two options in here

Option 1) Resetting the default graph.
tf.reset_default_graph()
x = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, shape=(None, 2))
(y, _, _, _) = tfl.lattice_layer(x, lattice_sizes=(2, 2))

with tf.Session() as sess:
  sess.run(tf.global_variables_initializer())
  print(sess.run(y, feed_dict={x: [[0.0, 0.0]]}))

Option 2) Use explicit variable scope.
x = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, shape=(None, 2))
with tf.variable_scope("test"):
  (y, _, _, _) = tfl.lattice_layer(x, lattice_sizes=(2, 2))

with tf.Session() as sess:
  sess.run(tf.global_variables_initializer())
  print(sess.run(y, feed_dict={x: [[0.0, 0.0]]}))

Hi Giedrius,

Address Query is a concept that is only valid within a single Thread
Network where all nodes by definition must share the same Mesh Local Prefix.

Are you saying you are trying to use Address Query on a Thread Network
where node A and C have different Mesh Local Prefixes?

If you want to use other prefixes in Thread, you need to add a Border
Router and use GUA addresses.  The is always exactly one Mesh Local Prefix
which must be shared by all nodes on the mesh.

@CiaranWoodward, thanks for submitting this proposal.  There have been a few other cursory requests to support the IEEE 802.15.4 MLME-SAP and MCPS-SAP.  However, it sounds like you have some working code, which is great.

I think you outlined the advantages of supporting the MLME-SAP and MCPS-SAP nicely.  Some of the reasons why OpenThread went with the current model are:

1. Support IEEE 802.15.4 radios that do not provide an MLME-SAP and MCPS-SAP.
2. Minimize MAC-layer resource requirements.  Thread uses a small subset of the IEEE 802.15.4 MAC sublayer.  Linking in a full MLME-SAP and MCPS-SAP implementation can pull in unnecessary code and memory requirements.
3. Allow for cross-layer optimizations.  In particular, OpenThread uses a single message buffer queue across the network and link layers to reduce memory consumption.  Similarly, OpenThread maintains a single neighbor table shared between the network and link layers.
4. More easily implement forward-looking features (i.e. proposed Thread enhancements as part of its Beyond Home initiative).  Some of these forward-looking features rely on lower-layer primitives which are not provided by IEEE 802.15.4 MLME-SAP or MCPS-SAP.

That said, I am supportive of finding a way to support the MLME-SAP and MCPS-SAP.  I'm aware of IEEE 802.15.4 platforms that completely offload the MAC to a separate peripheral.  I agree that having a different path down from MeshForwarder is not ideal but may be the more realistic solution going forward.

As for your question around Key ID Mode 2, I believe setting the Nonce outside IEEE 802.15.4's spec is "more correct."  As mentioned in Section 7.2.1.3:
> For both outgoing and incoming processing, the nonce value for the Key ID Mode 2 frames
input to the CCM* algorithm MUST be set to the ExtAddress value of the Key ID Mode 2
Device Descriptor instead of the value of aExtendedAddress of the originating node.

If the MAC implementation cannot support this Thread-specific behavior, this may be a roadblock to supporting Thread 1.1.  This may be something worth submitting in a [Thread Group JIRA](https://threadgroup.atlassian.net/).

I'm on a VPS too and you can simply deactivate the IPv6-Stack.
For example (Ubuntu 14.04 LTS):
open `/etc/sysctl.conf` with your favourite text editor and add the following lines at the end of the file!
```
net.ipv6.conf.all.disable_ipv6 = 1
net.ipv6.conf.default.disable_ipv6 = 1
net.ipv6.conf.lo.disable_ipv6 = 1
```

Then check the contents with `sudo sysctl -p`. It should show you the newly added lines of code.
To make sure everything works, you can add a 
`cat /proc/sys/net/ipv6/conf/all/disable_ipv6` which will report a `1` 
After that, try certbot renew again :)

Yeah, each sentence is padded into a fixed size, but the numbers in that `(544, 32)` tensor are vocabulary (`itos`) indices, not the actual vectors used in training. 

You then usually convert them to actual word vectors by passing that tensor through a `nn.Embedding`, which results in a `(544, 32, d)` tensor, where the first dimension is the sequence length, the second the batch size and last the vector dimension. Here is where the padding index is converted to a d-dimensional zero vector.

Here is a solution that @pcmoritz and I discussed that we think will cover this case as long as a task's `ray.put` objects fit in the object store.

The key issue is that by having a task do a `ray.put`, and then calling `ray.get` on other tasks that are dependent on the put value, we create a cyclic dependency. The caller waits on the return values of the other tasks, but the other tasks wait on the object that was put by the caller. If we lose that object but the caller is still waiting on the other tasks in a `ray.get`, then we will deadlock because we can't re-execute the caller.

We can avoid deadlock by ensuring that the object is available when the other tasks execute. We can do this by having the caller pin the object to its local object store. It's okay if that node dies, because then we will lose both the object and the caller, so we can simply re-execute the caller to reconstruct the object. If it's an option, we can also "pin" the object by replicating it to another node or writing it to disk, in case there's not enough memory in the local store.

Another option that is more radical is to break the cycle completely by splitting the caller into two tasks, one which returns the values that it called `ray.put` on and another that is the continuation. This would require us to introspect and mutate application code, and it's unlikely that we'll cover all cases in Python.

@cthoyt The reasons for the patch are:
- in general, we should not install something that's not required
- I'm working on a Conda package for PyBEL and the conda-forge package for `configparser` is only available for Python 2.7 and 3.4.

The last release of configparser is [3.5.0](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/configparser/3.5.0) and contains the updated configparser from Python 3.5 (as its version implies). To use it under Python 3.x, the syntax:
```
from backports import configparser
```
should be used. The only feature added after Python 3.3 is the `converters` argument for `configparser.ConfigParser()`, which pybel does not use. So probably a better fix would be using:
```
if sys.version_info < (3, ):
```

We could, but doing it that way is as you suspected, not quite right. We'd want to modify any events not in a list of events to not get those modifications. Behavior for `on_message` (and other d.py builtins) in your snippet is a little off.

Also, this wouldn't prevent someone from scheduling something in another cog (there's no way to actually do that, since I could just call whatever scheduler function your cog uses from mine. I could even modify it with `setattr`) I don't think modifying d.py internals for this is worth it, more just a thought on whether we should be enforcing unique 3rd party event names to avoid *accidentally* scheduling for another cog.

@mdiener The websockify command wrap mode is used to start a server and expose that service's listen port as a WebSocket port. In other words, if you were going to use wrap mode with mysql it would be to wrap the mysqld service, not the mysql client (the client would be something you write in JS that commincates over websockets to the server). Also, it is assumed that the websockify listen port matches the initial port of the service that is being wrapped. So using command mode would look more like this:

```
./websockify 127.0.0.1:8080 -- mysqld --port 8080 ...
```

However, the wrap command mode of websockify is mostly a development tool. As @CendioOssman mentioned, it's probably better to run mysqld separately and then have a separate websockify process to bridge to the mysqld port:

```
./websockify 127.0.0.1:8080 127.0.0.1:3306   # 3306 is the running mysqld listening port
```

Then you would connect using a WebSocket client (i.e. a browser) to 127.0.0.1:8080. This would triggers a normal TCP connection to 127.0.0.1:3306. The client would need to be some sort of mysql client (running in JavaScript) for anything interesting to work of course.
